input.userRequest: Recommend how Briefing Genie should summarize recovery work after a setup incident.

messages.content: You are Briefing Genie, a concise research briefing generator. Use only the provided synthetic source packet. Cite claims with source ids exactly as shown, such as A1 or B2. Do not invent citation ids, facts, or private data. Return a concise briefing suitable for a strategy review. First identify the distinct decision dimensions requested by the user, including risks, recommendations, tradeoffs, and approval boundaries. Use up to five claims to cover the highest-value dimensions before optimizing for brevity. When the packet contains multiple important risk types, include at least one claim for each type instead of collapsing to a single theme. Pair each claim with the strongest direct citation and narrow only the unsupported part, not the whole coverage area. Make the recommendation name the primary action plus the most important caveat or dependency from the sources.

User request: Recommend how Briefing Genie should summarize recovery work after a setup incident.

Source packet: Incident Recovery Communication Packet

Packet summary: Support, status-page, and customer-success notes about communicating recovery after failed installs.

Sources:

[S1] Support incident recap
Type: support recap
A failed installer release caused 31 setup tickets in two days. The highest-friction users had partial installs: they could not tell whether to rerun setup, clear credentials, or wait for a patched CLI.

The support recap lists partial installs as the biggest source of confusion. Users were unsure whether rerunning setup would corrupt local state or clear credentials. Agents asked for a recovery message that starts with symptoms and then gives one safe next action.

[S2] Status page draft
Type: status draft
The draft status update says the issue is resolved, but engineering has only verified recovery on macOS and Linux. Windows recovery steps are still being validated by the tooling team.

The status draft was written before Windows recovery validation finished. Engineering confirmed macOS and Linux, but the Windows path still had an unresolved credential-cache issue. The draft's word 'resolved' was flagged as too broad unless the environment caveat stayed visible.

[S3] Customer success guidance
Type: customer guidance
Customer success wants a concise message that separates who is affected, what action they should take, and which environments are still pending validation. They specifically asked not to claim universal resolution until Windows is verified.

Customer success asked for a message that separates affected users, recommended action, and pending validation. They wanted the Windows caveat in the first paragraph rather than a footnote. Their concern was that customers would forward the status update internally as proof of universal recovery.

[S4] Engineering fix note
Type: engineering note
The patched CLI adds a recovery command that checks stale credentials and cleans partial install state. The command is available in the release candidate, but docs screenshots still show the old installer flow.

The engineering note says the recovery command handles stale credentials and partial install cleanup. The command is available in the release candidate, while screenshots and public docs still show the old installer path. Engineering asked docs to update before broad promotion.

[S5] Docs update checklist
Type: docs checklist
The docs update checklist requires the new recovery command, credential cleanup instructions, and screenshots for the patched installer path. The docs owner marked Windows screenshots as pending. The checklist says customer communication should not point users to the old installer flow once the release candidate is promoted.

[S6] Support macro draft
Type: support macro
The support macro draft separates users into verified macOS, verified Linux, and pending Windows recovery paths. It gives partial-install users one safe command and tells Windows users that validation is still in progress. Support asked for the same segmentation to appear in any generated incident briefing.

output.title: How Briefing Genie should summarize recovery work after a setup incident
output.summary: Summarize recovery in a segmented, action-first format: lead with who is affected (especially partial installs), give one safe next action, and clearly state environment validation boundaries (macOS/Linux verified; Windows pending). Avoid “resolved everywhere” language until Windows recovery is validated, and align comms with updated docs once the release candidate is promoted.

claims.text: Lead the recovery summary with symptoms (especially partial installs) and provide exactly one clearly safe next action to reduce confusion about rerunning setup, credential clearing, or waiting for a patched CLI.

Do not claim the incident is universally “resolved”: engineering has only verified recovery on macOS and Linux, while Windows recovery steps are still being validated due to an unresolved credential-cache issue.

Structure the message into three blocks—who is affected, what action to take, and which environments are still pending validation—and place the Windows caveat in the first paragraph to prevent customers treating it as proof of universal recovery.

Reference the patched CLI’s new recovery command as the primary remediation because it checks stale credentials and cleans partial-install state, but note it is currently in the release candidate (and screenshots/docs still reflect the old installer flow).
Ensure the briefing mirrors support’s segmentation (verified macOS, verified Linux, pending Windows) and avoids pointing users to the old installer flow once the release candidate is promoted.

openQuestions: What is the explicit approval gate for changing public language from “mitigated/partial recovery” to “resolved,” and who signs off once Windows validation completes?
When will Windows recovery validation complete, and what interim guidance should Windows users follow given the credential-cache issue?
When will docs (including Windows screenshots) be updated to the new recovery command/installer path, and is comms blocked on that docs readiness checklist?
What exact “one safe command” string should Briefing Genie emit (and does it differ by OS), to stay consistent with support macros and engineering guidance?
output.recommendation: Publish an incident recovery summary that is segmented by OS and centered on a single safe recovery command for partial installs, while explicitly caveating that Windows recovery is still pending validation; only promote “resolved” messaging and point users to the new flow after Windows validation and the docs update (including screenshots) are complete.
